attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman who is 34 weeks pregnant presents to the Emergency Department with sudden onset of severe shortness of breath. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears severely distressed. Her pulse rate is 130/min, blood pressure 80/40 mmHg, respiratory rate 32/min, SpO2 85% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. Shortly after arrival, she becomes unresponsive and pulseless.

Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) is initiated immediately. The obstetric team has been called.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate modification to standard CPR technique in this patient?
options: Avoid chest compressions due to risk of fetal injury
Perform chest compressions at a rate of 80-100 per minute
Use a wedge to achieve left lateral tilt
Perform chest compressions 2 cm higher on the sternum
Place the patient in the left lateral position
attributes.explanation:
In a pregnant patient at 34 weeks gestation, the most appropriate modification to standard CPR technique is to use a wedge to achieve left lateral tilt. This is crucial because the gravid uterus can cause aortocaval compression when the patient is supine, significantly reducing venous return and cardiac output. If a wedge is not immediately available, manual left uterine displacement should be performed.

Performing chest compressions at a rate of 80-100 per minute is incorrect. The rate of chest compressions should be the same as in non-pregnant adults, which is 100-120 compressions per minute. The depth should also be the same, at 5-6 cm.

Placing the patient in the left lateral position is not recommended as it makes effective chest compressions difficult to perform. Instead, the uterus should be manually displaced to the left while the patient remains supine.

Avoiding chest compressions due to risk of fetal injury is absolutely incorrect. Effective maternal resuscitation is the best chance for fetal survival. Chest compressions should be performed without hesitation in maternal cardiac arrest.

Performing chest compressions 2 cm higher on the sternum is not necessary. Despite the elevated diaphragm in pregnancy, the hand position for chest compressions remains the same as in non-pregnant adults.

In addition to left uterine displacement, other important considerations in maternal resuscitation include early involvement of the obstetric and neonatal teams, consideration of perimortem caesarean section if initial resuscitation efforts are unsuccessful, and addressing reversible causes of arrest such as pulmonary embolism, which is more common in pregnancy. The basic principles of CPR, including high-quality chest compressions with minimal interruptions, remain the same as in non-pregnant adults.
learningPoints: Manual left uterine displacement should be performed during CPR in pregnant women with a fundal height at or above the umbilicus to relieve aortocaval compression.
The rate and depth of chest compressions in pregnant women should be the same as in non-pregnant adults.
Early involvement of the obstetric and neonatal teams is crucial in maternal cardiac arrest.
references: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG). Maternal Collapse in Pregnancy and the Puerperium: Green-top Guideline No. 56 [Internet]. London: RCOG; 2023. Available from: https://www.rcog.org.uk/guidance/browse-all-guidance/green-top-guidelines/maternal-collapse-in-pregnancy-and-the-puerperium-green-top-guideline-no-56/
